en.extract:
The swamp boubou (Laniarius bicolor), also known as the Gabon boubou, is a species of bird in the Malaconotidae or bushshrike family. It is native to western and southern Central Africa. In the north of their range, savannah thickets constitute an important part of their habitat, while in the south they are strongly associated with river systems and marshes, for which they are named. The pair bond appears to be maintained by duetting, which in the south is generally synchronous or overlapping. It is most similar to L. major major, with which it perhaps hybridizes, but the underpart plumage is immaculate white, while the female contributes a ratchet-like note to the duet.


Range and habitat
It is found in Angola, Botswana, Cameroon, Republic of the Congo, DRC, Gabon, Namibia, Zambia, and Zimbabwe. Its natural habitats are regenerating forest, moist to seasonally dry savannah thicket, coastal scrubland and mangroves, and ravine forests of the Angolan escarpment. In the south of its range it is confined to floodplain systems. Along these rivers and swamps they frequent dense, tangled riparian vegetation, edges of reedbeds, papyrus stands or clumps of water fig on islands.


Description and identification
The sexes are similar in appearance. They are large passerines that measure 23 to 25 cm in length. The upperparts are deep blue-black with a slight luster. The long, fluffy rump feathers have concealed white spots, giving the rump a pale appearance. The wings are black with white wing-stripes, and the underparts are pure white. The iris is dark brown, the bill and palate are black, and the legs and feet slate-blue. Juvenile birds have light dusky barring on the underside, and buffy-tipped and barred upperpart plumage. Immatures are duller than adults with buff-tipped wing-coverts and browner bills.
Their larger size, more slender bill, immaculate white underparts, and vocals distinguish them from the tropical boubou where they locally occur together. In those locations the southern race behaves as good species, but somewhat less so with the northern races which are sometimes merged with L. major.


Habits
They are monogamous and year-round territorial birds. They forage from ground level to the upper canopy, often working their way up before gliding down to adjacent vegetation. They also hawk insects, or flick ground debris like a thrush. Their food consists of arthropods, insects and worms, and on occasion small fruit. Pairs perform a duet which is initiated by a soft guttural sound, by either sex. The male then gives a short whistle, to which the female immediately replies with a harsh, ratchet-like kick-ick. Harsh clicking sounds are also given at dusk when approaching their roost. Vocals may be augmented by wing fripping in excited birds. Rump feathers are fluffed out during territorial threat or flight-song courtship displays.


Nesting
Nesting sites are widely spaced. Breeding occurs at any time of the year, though in the south, mainly in spring. The nest is a shallow cup of loosely woven twigs, placed some 2 to 3 meters above ground in a tangle, or in an ornamental bougainvillea shrub. Two eggs are laid, measuring 23 x 20 mm. They are pale cream or greenish in colour, and lightly speckled with rufous. Parasitism by the black cuckoo has been recorded. Moult occurs upon completion of the breeding period.


Races

There are three accepted races:

L. b. bicolor (Hartlaub, 1857), the "Gabon boubou"
Range: coastal Cameroon to Cabinda, Angola
Description: smallest and darkest race, sometimes with all-black secondaries
L. b. guttatus (Hartlaub, 1865)
Range: Republic of the Congo to Lubango, Angola
L. b. sticturus Hartlaub & Finsch, 1870, the "Okavango boubou"
Range: Kunene river, Kavango river and delta, and upper Zambezi catchment
Description: largest race with most white in the wing, and sometimes white-tipped rectrices


References


External links
Swamp boubou - Species text in The Atlas of Southern African Birds.

[[File:Swamp Boubou 110ND500 DSC7853.jpg|thumb|Perched in riparian vegetation, coastal Angola]]
The '''swamp boubou''' ('''''Laniarius bicolor'''''), also known as the '''Gabon boubou''', is a species of bird in the [[Malaconotidae]] or bushshrike family. It is native to western and southern [[Central Africa]]. In the north of their range, savannah thickets constitute an important part of their habitat, while in the south they are strongly associated with river systems and marshes, for which they are named. The pair bond appears to be maintained by duetting,<ref name="har">{{cite book |last1=Harris |first1=Tony |last2=Franklin |first2=Kim |title=Shrikes & bush-shrikes: including wood-shrikes, helmet-shrikes, flycatcher-shrikes, philentomas, batises and wattle-eyes |date=2000 |publisher=C. Helm |location=London |isbn=978-0-7136-3861-5 |pages=33–34, 102–103, 261–262}}</ref> which in the south is generally synchronous or overlapping.<ref name=hbw/> It is most similar to ''[[tropical boubou|L. major]] major'', with which it perhaps hybridizes, but the underpart plumage is immaculate white, while the female contributes a [[Ratchet (device)|ratchet]]-like note to the duet.

==Range and habitat==
It is found in [[Angola]], [[Botswana]], [[Cameroon]], [[Republic of the Congo]], [[Democratic Republic of the Congo|DRC]], [[Gabon]], [[Namibia]], [[Zambia]], and [[Zimbabwe]]. Its natural [[habitat]]s are regenerating forest, moist to seasonally dry [[savannah]] thicket, coastal [[scrubland]] and mangroves, and ravine forests of the Angolan escarpment.<ref name="har"/> In the south of its range it is confined to floodplain systems. Along these rivers and [[swamp]]s they frequent dense, tangled riparian vegetation,<ref name=wt>{{cite book |last=Tarboton |first=Warwick |title=A Guide to the Nests and Eggs of Southern African Birds |year=2001 |publisher=Struik |location=Cape Town |isbn=1-86872-616-9 |page=141}}</ref> edges of reedbeds, papyrus stands or clumps of [[Ficus verruculosa|water fig]] on islands.<ref name=cb>{{cite book|last=Masterson |first=A. N. B. |title=The complete book of southern African birds |year=1990 |publisher=Struik Winchester |location=Cape Town |isbn=0-9474-30-11-3 |page=416 |display-authors=etal}}</ref>

==Description and identification==
The sexes are similar in appearance. They are large [[passerine]]s that measure 23 to 25 cm in [[bird measurement|length]]. The upperparts are deep blue-black with a slight luster. The long, fluffy rump feathers have concealed white spots,<ref name="har"/> giving the rump a pale appearance.<ref name=cb/> The wings are black with white wing-stripes, and the underparts are pure white.<ref name="har"/> The iris is dark brown, the bill and palate are black, and the legs and feet slate-blue.<ref name=cb/> Juvenile birds have light dusky barring on the underside, and buffy-tipped and barred upperpart plumage.<ref name=cb/> Immatures are duller than adults with buff-tipped wing-coverts and browner bills.<ref name="har"/>

Their larger size, more slender bill, immaculate white underparts, and vocals distinguish them from the [[tropical boubou]] where they locally occur together. In those locations the southern race behaves as good species, but somewhat less so with the northern races<ref name=mps>{{cite book |last=Irwin |first=M. P. S. | pages = 346–347 |title=The Birds of Zimbabwe |year=1981 |publisher=Quest Publishing |location=Salisbury |isbn=086-9251-554}}</ref> which are sometimes merged with ''L. major''.<ref name="sinc">{{cite book |last1=Sinclair |first1=Ian |last2=Ryan |first2=Peter |title=Birds of Africa south of the Sahara |date=2010 |publisher=Struik Nature |location=Cape Town |isbn=978-1-77007-623-5 |pages=584–585 |edition=2nd}}</ref>

==Habits==
They are monogamous and year-round territorial birds. They forage from ground level to the upper canopy, often working their way up before gliding down to adjacent vegetation. They also hawk insects, or flick ground debris like a thrush. Their food consists of arthropods, insects and worms, and on occasion small fruit.<ref name="har"/> Pairs perform a duet which is initiated by a soft guttural sound, by either sex.<ref name="har"/> The male then gives a short whistle, to which the female immediately replies with a harsh, ratchet-like ''kick-ick''.<ref name=cb/> Harsh clicking sounds are also given at dusk when approaching their roost. Vocals may be augmented by wing fripping in excited birds. Rump feathers are fluffed out during territorial threat or flight-song courtship displays.<ref name="har"/>

==Nesting==
Nesting sites are widely spaced.<ref name=wt/> Breeding occurs at any time of the year,<ref name="har"/> though in the south, mainly in spring.<ref name=wt/> The nest is a shallow cup of loosely woven twigs, placed some 2 to 3 meters above ground in a tangle,<ref name="har"/> or in an [[ornamental plant|ornamental]] [[bougainvillea]] shrub. Two eggs are laid, measuring 23 x 20 mm.<ref name=wt/> They are pale cream or greenish in colour, and lightly speckled with rufous.<ref name="har"/> Parasitism by the [[black cuckoo]] has been recorded.<ref name="har"/> Moult occurs upon completion of the breeding period.

==Races==
[[File:Laniarius bicolor sticturus.jpg|thumb|right|175px|''Laniarius bicolor sticturus'' illustrated by [[Otto Finsch]], showing the three white-edged secondaries, and the white-tipped rectrices of this race]]
There are three accepted races:<ref name=hbw>{{cite web |last1=Fry |first1=H. |date = 2017 |title=Swamp Boubou (''Laniarius bicolor'') |website=Handbook of the Birds of the World Alive | publisher = Lynx Edicions, Barcelona | url =  http://www.hbw.com/node/60530 |access-date=19 April 2017}}</ref>
* ''L. b. bicolor'' <small>(Hartlaub, 1857)</small>, the "Gabon boubou"
:Range: coastal [[Cameroon]] to [[Cabinda Province|Cabinda]], Angola
:Description: smallest and darkest race, sometimes with all-black secondaries
* ''L. b. guttatus'' <small>(Hartlaub, 1865)</small>
:Range: [[Republic of the Congo]] to [[Lubango]], Angola
* ''L. b. sticturus'' <small>Hartlaub & Finsch, 1870</small>, the "Okavango boubou"
:Range: [[Kunene river]], [[Kavango river]] and [[Okavango Delta|delta]], and upper [[Zambezi River|Zambezi]] catchment
:Description: largest race with most white in the wing, and sometimes white-tipped rectrices<ref name="har"/>
